learning_objectives: Understand the relationship between Classes and Objects

Master the use of
Constructors for object initialization

Differentiate between Instance and
Static members

Correctly apply the 'this' keyword to resolve naming
conflicts
Understand Java's memory management and Garbage Collection basics
prerequisites: Basic knowledge of Java data types
introduction:
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a paradigm that organizes
software design around data, or objects, rather than functions and logic. In
this phase, we move from writing simple scripts to building modular, reusable
blueprints called Classes and creating functional entities called Objects. This
is the foundation of professional Java development.

concept_explanation.classes_and_objects:
A Class is a blueprint or template that defines the
attributes (data) and behaviors (methods) that an object of that type will have.
An Object is a specific instance of a class. For example, 'Car' is a class, but
your 'Blue 2023 Tesla Model 3' is an object.
concept_explanation.constructors:
A Constructor is
a special block of code that is called when an object is instantiated. Its main
purpose is to initialize the object's state. It has the same name as the class
and no return type.
concept_explanation.this_keyword:
The 'this' keyword is a reference
variable that refers to the current object. It is commonly used to distinguish
between class attributes and parameters with the same name.
concept_explanation.static_keyword:
The 'static' keyword means that a member (variable or method) belongs to the
class itself, rather than to any specific instance. All objects of the class
share the same static variable.
concept_explanation.garbage_collection:
Java manages memory
automatically. When an object is no longer reachable (no references point to
it), the Garbage Collector (GC) marks it for deletion to free up heap memory.

breakdown_of_syntax.class_keyword:
Used to
declare a new class structure.
breakdown_of_syntax.new_keyword:
Used to allocate memory on the
Heap for a new object.
breakdown_of_syntax.constructor_name:
Must exactly match the class name,
including case sensitivity.
breakdown_of_syntax.instance_variable:
Declared inside a class but
outside methods; represents the object's state.
breakdown_of_syntax.static_modifier:
Indicates
the member is shared across all instances of the class.

rules_and_key_points:
A class can have multiple constructors (Constructor
Overloading).

If no constructor is written, Java provides a hidden 'Default
Constructor'.

Static methods cannot access instance variables directly
because they don't belong to an object.

The 'this' keyword cannot be used in
a static context.

Objects are stored in the Heap memory; references are
stored in the Stack.

Constructors do not have a return type, not even
'void'.

Static variables are initialized only once when the class is
loaded.

The 'final' keyword can be used with instance variables to make them
constants.

Instance variables have default values (0, false, null), but local
variables do not.

Garbage collection is automatic but can be suggested using
System.gc(), though it's not guaranteed.

code_example_1.title:
Basic
Class and Object
code_example_1.description:
Creating a simple Dog class with attributes
and a method.

description:
A BankAccount class demonstrating shared class data using the
static keyword.

steps: Memory is allocated on the Heap for a new Dog object.
The Dog constructor is called with arguments 'Buddy' and 3.

Inside the
constructor, 'this.name' refers to the Heap object's name field, which is
assigned 'Buddy'.
The 'this.age' field is assigned the value 3.

The
constructor completes, and the memory address (reference) of the object is
returned.

The reference is stored in the local variable 'myDog' on the
Stack.

memory_visualization.stack:
Contains the reference variable
'myDog' which holds the memory address (e.g., @0x101).
best_practices:
Always initialize instance variables through
constructors.

Use 'this' keyword in constructors to avoid confusion between
parameters and fields.

Keep instance variables private (Encapsulation) and
use public methods to access them.

Use static variables for constants or
shared counters only.

Follow PascalCase for Class names and camelCase for
variable/method names.

frequently_asked_questions.question:
Can a
class have more than one constructor?
frequently_asked_questions.answer:
Yes, this is called
Constructor Overloading. You can have multiple constructors as long as their
parameter lists are different.

frequently_asked_questions.question:
What is the default value of
an uninitialized object reference?
frequently_asked_questions.answer:
The default value is 'null',
meaning it does not point to any object in memory.

memory_visualization.summary:
Phase 5
introduced the core pillars of Java objects. We learned that classes are
blueprints, objects are instances, and constructors set the initial state. We
also explored the 'static' keyword for shared data and how Java manages memory
through the Stack, Heap, and Garbage Collector.
key_takeaways: Heap memory stores objects; Stack stores references.
memory_visualization.preview_of_next_topic:
In Phase 6, we will explore 'Inheritance', learning
how classes can inherit attributes and behaviors from other classes to promote
code reuse.

questions_and_answers.question: What is a constructor in Java?
questions_and_answers.answer:
A constructor is a special method
used to initialize objects. It is called automatically when an object is created
with the 'new' keyword. It must have the same name as the class and cannot have
a return type.

questions_and_answers.question:
Explain the
difference between a static method and an instance method.
questions_and_answers.answer:
A static
method belongs to the class and can be called without creating an object. An
instance method belongs to an object and requires an instance of the class to be
invoked. Static methods cannot access instance variables directly.

questions_and_answers.question:
Implement a class 'Product' with a
parameterized constructor and use 'this' to assign values.

questions_and_answers.answer:
The error is 'non-static variable x
cannot be referenced from a static context'. Since main is static, it cannot
access the instance variable 'x' without creating an instance of the Test
class.

The output is 2. Because
'count' is static, it is shared across all instances. Every time a new Counter
object is created, the same static variable is incremented.

questions_and_answers.question:
Explain and implement a 'Copy Constructor' in Java for a
class 'Point' with x and y coordinates.
questions_and_answers.answer:
A copy constructor is a
constructor that creates a new object using the values of an existing object of
the same class.

questions_and_answers.question:
Write a program that uses a static block to
initialize a static list of settings.

Create a 'Student' class where each
student is automatically assigned a unique ID starting from 1001. Use a static
variable to track the next ID.

answer.question:
What is the return
type of a constructor?
Which area of memory stores the actual object data?

What happens if you define a constructor with a 'void' return
type?

options.B:
It becomes a
regular method and is no longer a constructor.

this cannot be used in
static context

answer.question:
Which keyword is used to refer to the current
class instance?
When is a static block executed?

options.A:
Every time an
object is created.
options.C:
Once, when the
class is first loaded into memory.

answer.question:
What is the best approach for managing unique
serial numbers for objects?

options.A:
Use a local variable in the
constructor.
options.C:
Use a static
variable incremented in the constructor.
options.D:
Pass a new number from the main
method every time.
